site.title: Research and Robotics
home.hero.summary: I build semantic, cognitive, and robotic systems that connect formal models with real-world autonomous behavior based on hypergraph models.
home.overview.title: Research, education, and creative directions
home.intro.0: Welcome to my personal page. My work focuses on cognitive systems, artificial intelligence, and robotics, with a strong emphasis on hypergraph-based semantic models and their use in autonomous platforms.
home.intro.1: I am currently an Assistant Lecturer at Szechenyi Istvan University and a Researcher at Obuda University in Hungary. My interests span advanced cognitive models, intelligent robotic systems, and the specification-driven analysis of complex environments.
home.intro.2: This site brings together my research directions, education activities, supervised students, and selected creative interests in one place.

page.thesisFaq.title: Thesis FAQ and key guidance
page.thesisFaq.summary: A short orientation page for thesis work, practical expectations, and the most important things to know before starting.
page.thesisFaq.start.0: Choose a topic that is narrow enough to finish well, but meaningful enough to demonstrate real engineering or research value.
page.thesisFaq.start.1: Clarify the expected outcome early: literature review, prototype, experiment, software artifact, dataset, or system integration.
page.thesisFaq.start.2: Agree on the language of the work and presentation in advance, especially if the topic is developed in both Hungarian and English contexts.
page.thesisFaq.start.3: Start collecting references from the beginning so the final writing phase is not separated from the technical work.
page.thesisFaq.workflow.0: Keep progress visible through short updates, versioned files, and clearly named milestones.
page.thesisFaq.workflow.1: Bring partial drafts early. It is much easier to improve a rough chapter or prototype than to repair a full late-stage submission.
page.thesisFaq.workflow.2: Document decisions, assumptions, and open questions while working, not only at the end.
page.thesisFaq.workflow.3: When software is involved, keep the repository runnable and reproducible so evaluation and continuation stay straightforward.
page.thesisFaq.delivery.0: The written thesis should explain the problem, context, method, implementation, evaluation, and conclusions in a coherent structure.
page.thesisFaq.delivery.1: Figures, tables, and experimental results should support the argument, not just decorate it.
page.thesisFaq.delivery.2: Code, experiments, and references should be organized so another person can understand what was done and why.
page.thesisFaq.delivery.3: A strong thesis shows both technical execution and reflective thinking about limitations, tradeoffs, and future work.
page.thesisFaq.tips.0: Do not leave writing to the final weeks. Write continuously alongside the implementation.
page.thesisFaq.tips.1: Make the scope realistic. A finished and well-evaluated smaller system is better than an oversized unfinished one.
page.thesisFaq.tips.2: Back up everything: text, code, figures, data, and notes.
page.thesisFaq.tips.3: Ask questions early when something is unclear. Small uncertainties tend to become large delays if they remain implicit.
